section.title: Wives and Husbands
section.sectionRationale: Having called the church to be filled with the Spirit and to submit to one another out of reverence for Christ, Paul now works that mutual submission out in the first and deepest relationship of the household: marriage. This is not an isolated set of rules but the flowering of the Spirit-filled life of chapter five, and it reaches its climax not in a command about spouses but in a revelation about Christ and the church. Marriage, Paul says, has always been pointing beyond itself to the union of the Redeemer and His people.
section.thesis: In the fear of Christ, wives are called to submit to their husbands as the church submits to Christ its head, and husbands are called to love their wives as Christ loved the church and gave Himself up for her; and the profound mystery of one flesh, spoken from the beginning, was all along about Christ and the church.
section.frameNote: Four movements: the governing call to submit to one another out of reverence for Christ; the wife and the pattern of the church's submission to Christ; the husband and the pattern of Christ's self-giving love for the church; and the marriage mystery unveiled, that this union was always about Christ and the church.

units.label: Submit to one another
units.note: the governing call, out of reverence for Christ

verses.text: {{hypotasso|Submit}} to one another out of reverence for Christ.

units.label: Wives and the church's submission to Christ

verses.text: Wives, submit to your husbands as to the Lord.
For the husband is the {{kephale|head}} of the wife as Christ is the head of the church, His body, of which He is the {{soter|Savior}}.
Now as the church submits to Christ, so also wives should submit to their husbands in everything.

units.label: Husbands and Christ's love for the church
units.note: he gave Himself up for her

verses.text: Husbands, {{agapao|love}} your wives, just as Christ loved the church and gave Himself up for her
to {{hagiazo|sanctify}} her, cleansing her by the washing with water through the word,
and to present her to Himself as a glorious church, without stain or wrinkle or any such blemish, but holy and blameless.
In the same way, husbands ought to love their wives as their own bodies. He who loves his wife loves himself.
Indeed, no one ever hated his own body, but he nourishes and cherishes it, just as Christ does the church.
For we are members of His body.

units.label: The marriage mystery, Christ and the church

verses.text: "For this reason a man will leave his father and mother and be united to his wife, and the two will become one flesh."
This {{mysterion|mystery}} is profound, but I am speaking about Christ and the church.
Nevertheless, each one of you also must love his wife as he loves himself, and the wife must respect her husband.

hypotasso.gloss: To submit, to place oneself under, to yield in willing order. Originally a military term for arranging under a commander, in the New Testament it describes the voluntary taking of one's ordered place for the sake of another. In verse 21 it is reciprocal, submit to one another, and it governs the whole household code that follows. It is never coerced from without but offered from within, out of reverence for Christ, and it is patterned on the church's own submission to her Lord.

kephale.gloss: Head. The word carries the sense both of a source or origin and of one who holds a position of authority and responsibility, as a head over a body. Paul sets the husband's headship over the wife directly alongside Christ's headship over the church, His body. Whatever its precise nuance, the headship in view is defined entirely by the next paragraph: it is the headship of one who gives himself up, nourishes, and cherishes, never a licence to dominate.

soter.gloss: Savior, deliverer, the one who rescues and preserves. Christ is the head of the church precisely as the Savior of His body, the one who gave Himself to redeem her. The title stands as a quiet limit on the whole analogy: Christ's headship is saving, self-sacrificing headship, and though no husband can save his wife, the pattern of his love is drawn from the Savior who laid down His life.

agapao.gloss: To love with a self-giving, sacrificial love that seeks the good of the other regardless of cost. This is the love that God shows in giving His Son and that Christ shows in giving Himself up for the church. When Paul commands husbands to love their wives, he defines the word at once by the cross: just as Christ loved the church and gave Himself up for her. It is love measured not by feeling but by sacrifice.

hagiazo.gloss: To sanctify, to set apart as holy, to make clean and consecrated to God. Christ loves the church not only to rescue her but to sanctify her, cleansing her by the washing of water through the word and presenting her to Himself radiant and blameless. The husband's love, echoing this, is a love that seeks the holiness and flourishing of the other, not merely his own comfort.

mysterion.gloss: A mystery, a truth once hidden and now revealed by God. Paul takes the words of Genesis about a man and wife becoming one flesh and declares that this profound mystery was all along speaking of Christ and the church. Marriage is not merely illustrated by the gospel; from the beginning it was designed to display the union of the Redeemer with His people, a signpost planted in Eden that points to the wedding of the Lamb.

basic.context: Paul turns from the Spirit-filled life to the home, beginning with husbands and wives. Wives are to submit to their husbands as the church submits to Christ. Husbands are to love their wives as Christ loved the church, giving Himself up for her to make her holy and radiant. A husband who loves his wife loves himself, for the two are one flesh. Then Paul lifts the whole picture up: the ancient words about a man and woman becoming one flesh were always about Christ and the church. Marriage is a living picture of the gospel.
basic.hermeneutics: The passage opens with a heading in verse 21, submit to one another out of reverence for Christ, and then applies it to wife and husband. The weight falls not on the roles themselves but on their model: the church's submission to Christ and Christ's sacrificial love for the church. Read rightly, the passage is less a set of household rules than an unveiling of the marriage between Christ and His people, with human marriage as its shadow.
basic.originalLanguages: The whole section is governed by hypotasso, to submit, to take one's ordered place willingly, first said of all believers to one another. The husband is the kephale, head, as Christ is head of the church, and Christ is her soter, Savior. Husbands are to agapao, to love with self-giving love, as Christ loved and gave Himself to hagiazo, to sanctify, the church. Paul then calls the one-flesh union a mysterion, a revealed mystery, that all along spoke of Christ and the church.
basic.typology: Marriage itself is the type here. The one-flesh union of Genesis 2, spoken at the dawn of creation, was designed from the beginning to point forward to the union of Christ and His church. The husband foreshadows the self-giving love of the Redeemer; the wife and her glad response foreshadow the church cleansed and presented to her Lord.
basic.christ: Christ fills the passage. He is the head and Savior of the church, the one who loved her and gave Himself up for her, who cleanses her by the word and will present her to Himself radiant, holy, and without blemish. Every command to husbands is measured by His cross, and the whole mystery of marriage is declared to be about Him and His bride.
basic.god: God is the author of marriage, who from the beginning built into the union of man and woman a picture of a greater union. He is the God who gives His Son for a people and who purposes to present that people spotless and glorious. The passage shows a God whose plan for the home flows out of His plan of redemption.
basic.crossReferences: Genesis 2 gives the one-flesh union Paul quotes. Colossians 3 gives the parallel household code. Revelation 19 shows the marriage of the Lamb, the church made ready as a bride. 2 Corinthians 11 pictures the church betrothed as a pure virgin to Christ.
basic.oneStory: The story that began with a marriage in the garden ends with a marriage at the end of all things, the wedding of the Lamb. Between them runs the long courtship of God for His people, Israel called His bride, and the church bought with the blood of Christ. Every faithful marriage is a small living echo of that great romance, and points to the day when the bride is presented to her Lord without stain or wrinkle.

context.literary: These verses stand at the head of the household code that runs from 5:21 to 6:9, addressing wives and husbands, children and parents, slaves and masters. The code flows directly out of the call to be filled with the Spirit in 5:18, of which mutual submission in verse 21 is one mark. The structure pairs each address to the one in the ordered place, wife, child, slave, with an address to the one holding authority, husband, father, master, and grounds each in the Lord.
context.historical: Household codes were a familiar form in the ancient world, moral teaching on how the members of a household should relate. What is striking in Paul is not the form but the content: he addresses wives, children, and slaves as responsible persons in their own right, and he lays the heaviest weight of all on the husband, commanding a self-sacrificing love unheard of in the surrounding culture. The call for a husband to give himself up for his wife, as Christ did for the church, reframes the whole institution.
context.canonical: The passage joins Colossians 3:18-19, 1 Peter 3:1-7, and Genesis 2:24 as the Bible's central teaching on marriage. Its vision of the church as the bride of Christ links it to the prophetic marriage imagery of Hosea and Isaiah and to the wedding of the Lamb in Revelation 19 and 21, making human marriage a thread in the whole Scripture's tapestry of covenant love.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To shape Christian marriage by the gospel, calling wives to a glad, ordered submission modeled on the church's response to Christ and husbands to a costly, sanctifying love modeled on Christ's self-giving for the church, and above all to reveal that the one-flesh union was always meant to display the union of Christ and His people.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The commands to submit and to love are genuinely prescriptive, addressed to Christian wives and husbands. Yet the deepest note of the passage is a declaration: this mystery is about Christ and the church. The abiding application is that marriage is to be lived as a picture of the gospel, and that all who are in Christ, married or not, are the bride He loved and gave Himself for.

debates.question: How should the household code and the call to submission be understood?

views.label: The complementarian reading: ordered, self-giving headship modeled on Christ
views.case: On this reading verse 21 introduces mutual submission in general, but Paul then applies it in an ordered way: the wife is called to submit to her husband as the church submits to Christ, and the husband is called to a headship defined wholly by Christ's self-sacrifice. Head, kephale, carries a note of loving authority and responsibility, but authority to serve, not to dominate. The husband's role is the harder calling, to love as Christ loved the church and gave Himself up for her, spending himself for her holiness and flourishing. The distinct roles are seen as rooted in creation, verse 31 quoting Genesis 2, and as together displaying the gospel.

views.label: The egalitarian or mutual-submission reading: verse 21 governs the whole, head as source
views.case: On this reading verse 21, submit to one another, is the true heading, and everything that follows is an outworking of a mutual submission that both spouses owe each other. The address to wives in verse 22 has no verb of its own in Greek but borrows submit from verse 21, which is taken to show that submission is shared. Head, kephale, is understood primarily as source or origin, as Christ is the source of the church's life, rather than as a rank of authority. The emphasis falls on the husband laying down all self-assertion in Christlike love, so that in practice each yields to the other.

debates.bearing: Both sides agree that the husband's calling is Christlike, sacrificial love, never domination, and that the wife is honored as a fellow heir of grace. The passage's own weight falls not on settling the precise shape of the roles but on the pattern behind them: Christ and the church. Whatever one concludes about headship and submission, the point Paul drives home is that marriage exists to display the self-giving love of the Redeemer and the glad response of His people.

study.originalLanguages: The section is held together by hypotasso, to submit, to take one's ordered place willingly rather than under compulsion, first spoken of all believers to one another in verse 21 and then applied to the wife. The husband is the kephale, head, a word that can carry the sense of source and of responsible authority, set in direct parallel with Christ as head of the church, His body, of which He is the soter, Savior. To husbands Paul gives the command to agapao, to love with the self-giving love of the cross, just as Christ loved the church and gave Himself up for her, and the goal of that love is to hagiazo, to sanctify, to set apart holy and to cleanse. Finally Paul takes the one-flesh words of Genesis and calls them a mysterion, a truth long hidden and now unveiled, that was all along speaking of Christ and the church.

typology.fulfillment: The joining of man and wife into one flesh, spoken at creation, is revealed to have been a signpost pointing to the union of Christ and His church, the mystery Paul unveils in verse 32.
Fulfilled and exceeded in Christ, who loved the church and gave Himself up for her, the pattern to which every Christian husband is called.
Fulfilled in the church cleansed by the word and presented to Christ without stain or wrinkle, radiant and blameless, at the wedding of the Lamb in Revelation 19 and 21.

study.christ: This passage places Christ and His love at the center of marriage. He is the head of the church, but a head who is her Savior, who loved her and gave Himself up for her. His love is not sentiment but sacrifice: He gave Himself to sanctify her, to cleanse her by the washing of water through the word, and to present her to Himself as a glorious church, holy and blameless, without stain or wrinkle or any blemish. Every husband's love is measured against this cross, and every wife's honor mirrors the church's response to her Lord. Paul then lifts the veil: the one-flesh mystery of Eden was about Christ and the church all along. The gospel is a wedding, and marriage is its picture.
study.god: God is the maker of marriage and the author of the mystery it displays. From the first joining of man and wife in the garden, He was speaking of the union He purposed between His Son and a redeemed people. He is the God who gives His Son to win a bride and who will not rest until she stands before Him radiant and spotless. The passage reveals a God whose design for the most intimate of human bonds was always to preach the gospel of self-giving love and faithful covenant.

crossReferences.note: The one-flesh union Paul quotes in verse 31, the creation ordinance that he reveals was pointing to Christ and the church.
The parallel household code in the sister letter, wives submit and husbands love, without harshness.
The marriage of the Lamb, the bride made ready, the fulfillment of the church presented to Christ.
Paul betroths the church as a pure virgin to Christ, close kin to the presenting of the bride in verse 27.

study.oneStory: The Bible opens with a marriage in a garden and closes with a marriage at the end of all things, the wedding of the Lamb and His bride. Between them God courts a people for Himself: Israel called His bride, unfaithful yet pursued, and then the church bought with the blood of His Son. Here Paul reveals that the one-flesh union spoken at creation was always a picture of this greater union. Every faithful Christian marriage is a small echo of the gospel, a living parable of the love that gave itself up to make a bride holy and to present her spotless forever.

quiz.q: What is the governing call that opens this section in verse 21?

options.text: Submit to one another out of reverence for Christ.
options.note: Verse 21 heads the whole household code with mutual submission grounded in the fear of Christ.

options.text: Wives must obey husbands who have earned their trust.
options.note: The opening call is broader and mutual, submit to one another, and rooted in reverence for Christ.

options.text: Each person should assert their own rights first.
options.note: The call is the opposite of self-assertion; it is willing submission out of reverence for Christ.

options.text: Households should follow the customs of the surrounding culture.
options.note: Paul grounds the household not in custom but in reverence for Christ and the pattern of the gospel.

quiz.q: How does Paul define the husband's love for his wife?

options.text: As the self-giving love with which Christ loved the church and gave Himself up for her.
options.note: Verse 25 measures the husband's love by the cross, sacrificial and for her good.

options.text: As a warm affection that seeks his own comfort.
options.note: The love in view is agapao, sacrificial, seeking her holiness, not the husband's ease.

options.text: As firm authority that keeps the wife in her place.
options.note: The command is to love and give himself up, not to dominate or control.

options.text: As an optional ideal for especially devout husbands.
options.note: It is a direct command to all husbands, patterned on Christ's love for the church.

quiz.q: Why does Christ give Himself up for the church, according to verses 26 and 27?

options.text: To sanctify and cleanse her by the word and present her holy and blameless.
options.note: His love aims at her holiness, cleansing her and presenting her radiant, without stain or wrinkle.

options.text: To leave her as she is, requiring no change.
options.note: His love cleanses and sanctifies; it does not leave her unchanged.

options.text: To reward her for making herself worthy first.
options.note: He cleanses her by His own washing through the word; the holiness is His gift, not her achievement.

options.text: To display His power without any cost to Himself.
options.note: He gave Himself up for her; the love is costly, measured by the cross.

quiz.q: What does Paul reveal the one-flesh union of Genesis to be about?

options.text: A profound mystery that all along spoke of Christ and the church.
options.note: Verse 32 unveils the one-flesh union as a signpost to the union of Christ and His people.

options.text: Only the practical arrangement of an ancient household.
options.note: Paul lifts it far higher: it is a mystery about Christ and the church.

options.text: A rule that no longer applies to believers.
options.note: Paul upholds the union and reveals its deeper meaning; he does not set it aside.

options.text: A picture with no connection to the gospel.
options.note: The whole point of verse 32 is that it is about Christ and the church.

quiz.q: What do both readings of this passage agree on regarding the husband's calling?

options.text: That it is Christlike, sacrificial love, never domination.
options.note: Complementarian and egalitarian readings alike insist the husband is called to lay down his life, not to dominate.

options.text: That the husband may rule his wife as he sees fit.
options.note: Neither reading permits domination; both point to Christ's self-giving love as the standard.

options.text: That the husband has no particular calling in marriage.
options.note: Both readings give the husband the weighty calling to love as Christ loved the church.

options.text: That love is merely a feeling the husband cannot control.
options.note: The love commanded is measured by the cross, a self-giving act, not a passing feeling.

quiz.q: According to verses 28 to 30, why should a husband love his wife as his own body?

options.text: Because the two are one flesh, so in loving her he loves himself, as Christ loves the church, His body.
options.note: The one-flesh union means his wife's good is his own good, mirroring Christ and the church.

options.text: Because a well-treated wife will serve him better.
options.note: Paul's reason is the one-flesh union and Christ's love, not the husband's self-interest.

options.text: Because the law demands it under threat.
options.note: The ground is the union of one flesh and Christ's love, not mere legal threat.

options.text: Because it makes no real difference how he treats her.
options.note: Paul says a man nourishes and cherishes his own body, just as Christ does the church.

questions: Paul roots the whole household in reverence for Christ. How would seeing your home as an act of worship change the way you relate to those closest to you?
Christ loved the church by giving Himself up for her. If Christ's cross is the measure of a husband's love, what would that kind of love cost you in your own relationships?
Christ cleanses and sanctifies His bride to present her radiant and blameless. How does it comfort you that Christ is committed not only to save you but to make you holy?
Paul reveals that marriage was always meant to picture Christ and the church. How does knowing you are part of the bride Christ gave Himself for shape your sense of worth and belonging?
Whether married or single, you are loved by a Savior who gave Himself for you. How might that settled love free you to serve and to submit to others out of reverence for Christ?

translationNotes.note: In the Greek, verse 22 has no verb of its own; wives submit borrows the verb from verse 21, submit to one another. This grammatical link is part of why interpreters differ over how far mutual submission governs what follows.

Head renders kephale, a word that can carry the sense of source or origin as well as responsible authority; the passage defines the headship in view entirely by Christ, who is the Savior who gave Himself for the church.

This verse quotes Genesis 2:24, the creation ordinance of marriage; the straight double quotation marks preserve the citation, which Paul then unveils in verse 32 as a mystery about Christ and the church.

meta.notes: BSB text for Ephesians 5:21-33 reproduced as supplied. Verse 31 quotes Genesis 2:24, kept with straight double quotes. No em or en dashes used; punctuation kept clean per the style rule.
